engineering.translation: The questioner sharpens his pushback. He labels the senior's speech vyāmiśra — mixed, equivocal. He is not seeking nuance; he is demanding one decisive answer, niścitya, the single course by which he may attain śreyas, the highest good. This is the engineer who has heard the principal say one thing in a design review and another in a stand-up, and who now refuses to act on apparent contradiction. Per the Advaita commentary, the questioner has misread the senior's words as mixed when they were actually sequenced; per the Vishishtadvaita reading, the questioner has not yet seen how the two threads integrate. Either way, his pushback compels the senior to answer cleanly in 3.3. The seeker's plea for one clear instruction is legitimate when the prior speech really was mixed, and corrosive when it was not.
engineering.concrete_scenario: A team lead has been receiving two opposing signals from the principal across the last three days. On Monday, in a Notion design review, the principal said the right move was to delay the Postgres migration and get the data model right. On Wednesday, in a GitHub stand-up thread, he said the team had to deploy by Friday or the customer would churn. The lead reads both messages again at 9 PM Thursday and types directly to the principal on Slack: 'I cannot operate on these two together. Monday says delay; Wednesday says deploy. Tell me which is the real instruction. I will run the rollout tonight or push the ETA, but I need a single answer, not two.' The principal sees the message, recognises that prior communication had been muddled (whether by design or accident does not matter to the lead), and is now forced to choose. The lead is not being insubordinate. He is doing what 3.2 names: refusing to act on contradictory speech and demanding the single decisive line. The senior's response — 3.3 — will distinguish the two paths cleanly.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader uses this verse to justify always demanding one answer when situations are genuinely complex. Some engineering choices are inherently contingent — deployment depends on whether a canary holds, architecture depends on whether load is bursty or sustained. Demanding 'one decisive answer' in those cases asks for false simplification. The verse does not endorse impatience with real contingency; it endorses refusing to act on prior speech that was actually mixed.
engineering.counter_example: When the senior's two messages were actually consistent and the questioner has misheard them as mixed, the demand for 'one answer' is itself the failure mode. The Advaita reading specifically says this is what happened to the questioner. The diagnostic: re-read the prior speech with the assumption that it might be sequenced. If on careful re-reading the two messages reconcile, the demand for one answer was premature. Only when the speech was genuinely mixed is the demand legitimate.
engineering.implication: When a colleague demands 'one decisive answer', stop and audit your prior communication. If you were genuinely mixed, give them the answer now. If you were sequenced and they misheard, explain the sequence — never flatten the teaching to satisfy the demand.
engineering.quotable_line: When a team member demands one decisive answer, audit your prior speech first; do not flatten the teaching to satisfy the demand.

iterations.mutation: v0: 3.2 sharpens 3.1's pushback. Engineering layer maps the questioner's plea for ekam to a team lead refusing to act on apparently contradictory communication from the principal across two days. Concrete scenario uses Slack to fix the moment; counter-example carves out the case where the speech was actually sequenced (the Advaita reading) and the questioner has misheard. Tags: decision-paralysis, engineer-paralysis, knowledge-action-integration.
